The pain threshold means that essentially human beings, as we're built, when we're feeling severe pain or discomfort or angst, we will tend to act. And if it really is very severe, we will act. We will act even when we're not certain if we're in pain. We'll move out of pain very quickly to try to get back into at least, if not pleasure, at least feeling no more pain. So now, there's a very short description of pain, but I'll dig into it in a few moments. I want to show you now, I want to show you this graph, I think it's really going to help you out. So imagine, and you know my famous skills as an artist, right? Thanks to, let me missis Rossick and my kid, the God teacher. So here's the deal, right? This is, imagine one of those old-fashioned scales. And this is what it's referred to as the beam, the long beam. And then on this beam, you can bear weight on this side and that side. And what else you need is a fulcrum in the middle. So this is your fulcrum, your fulcrum, this is your beam, right? Now, when you weigh something, right, let's say you have an object on this side, this is your object. You have to apply a force to this side to balance this or else it tips over, right? So imagine in a sales encounter, right? When someone enters an encounter, they always enter with preconceived notions, right? Those preconceived notions consist of reasons they should be buying now. They walk into, that's why they're probably there, there's some reasons why they should buy now, both on a logical and emotional level. So they walk in with reasons to buy now, which is probably why they clicked on your website or walked in your door, answered the phone, or if you talk to and get qualified. But on the same time, they also concerns, they have reasons not to buy now. They have reasons to buy now and reasons not to buy now. And at the beginning of the scale, of the sale, what happens is that they might, they're probably not balanced 100%, but remember when you have something like this, it's not like one little penny weight would tip the scale either way. When you have a big scale like that, you might just have it maybe leaning slightly to the slightly down angle or slightly, or not even, they're almost balanced. And that represents like a four or five or a six on the certainty scale. So if someone's like at a five or a six or a four, it's around there, right? They're in the middle, it's sitting on the fence. Basically what it means, they enter the encounter with a match of reasons to buy in their own, the preconceived notions to buy and not to buy. They're basically matched. So the question is, will someone buy at this moment in time? Well, the answer is actually depends. Now let's just say, we always have positive intent. So if someone thinks it's going to hurt them, not going to help them, they're not going to buy no matter what. Let's just assume, maybe it is just like a slight extra positive here, right? And these negatives are really, they're bouncing, but they believe the product will help them. But they're kind of balanced. Will they buy? The answer is maybe. I'll tell you why. Because this fulcrum represents their action threshold. Me, the position of the fulcrum represents the action threshold. Someone like myself that has a very low action threshold. What it means is my fulcrum is not in the middle. My fulcrum is over here. So what happens is every positive has massive weight to it. It has huge pressure on the scale and every negative, well, it doesn't have quite that same impact with gravity. The force it exerts is not equal to the force this exerts. Because my fulcrum is over at skewed to the left here. That's someone with a low action threshold. And the reason, and by this movie, deeper here, what's happening is because of the movies I'm running, as a low action threshold guy, I run the long positive movie, the short negative movie, which results in this fulcrum being closer to the left. My father on the other hand, right? In his case, his fulcrum is where? Almost all the way over here to the right. So all these positives, dad, I'll pay for it. You need it. You can't enjoy your life anymore. You feel trapped. You're the woman you love. My mom, who takes care of it, she'll like me so much. But it'll be great for all of you. And there's really no risk. Why not? All of those positives measured against what's the negatives? We'll be changing our location. We're used to where we are. We've been here a whole like, they're not, to my dad, those minor things. What if I don't like it? What if something goes wrong? He's running all these things. This has massive weight. Even it's one or two negatives. And it corrupts his ability, pollutes his decision-making strategy. You get that? That's what's happening with someone with a very high action threshold. So what we do with the straight line system is the first thing we do is, as you move so and now watch. So here's your straight line down here. This is your open. This is your closed. Your boundaries above and below, right? As you move so and down the straight line, from the open to the close, everything you're saying, everything you're doing, every document you hand your prospect, every website you direct them to, every utterance out of your mouth, every body language gesture, every tonal pattern you run that overlays your words. Everything is all designed to essentially knock out negatives and build up positives as you move someone down the straight line. That's what's happening. You are knocking out negatives, knocking out negatives, building up positives, building up positives. And then as you do that, what happens? Well, at some point here, you ask for the order, right? I mean, over here, right? You ask for the order. And by the time you've done that, by all the things that you've already said now, the rapport you've built, the intelligence you've gathered, the case that you've made, this airtight logical case you've framed, well, guess what? You've knocked out a bunch of negatives and you've certainly built out some positives here, right? What does he buy? Well, if it's someone like me with a very low actress, I say, yeah, you're probably going to get me right there. Because what happens is the silver, boom, scales easy to tip. Someone like my father, not that much, but the point is, have you made progress? Yes or no? See, this is when people get screwed up. When you have these tougher buyers, what you're not realizing is that you're actually making massive progress as you're getting older, not interested. Let me think about it. Let me call you back by the time you're here. All that objection hopping is simply a function of someone not being certain of yet is still too many negatives and versus positives in relation always to their action threshold. Does that make sense? All buyers, all prospects are not created equal. There's prospects like me where I don't have to have a zillion in one positive and virtually no negatives. That's not me. I could have eight positives and seven negatives. I'll say, if I don't think it'll kill me and I think it'll help me in some way. My dad, no, no, no, no, no, he's got to be damn sure of it. But the point is when my father says, it sounds good. Let me think about it. You've made massive progress. It's just below the water line, so to speak. It's not visible yet, but progress has most certainly been made. Okay, you get this is very important because what happens is it sales people when they get hit with those objections, objection, they get the moralized. They start to feel like, oh, it's a lost cause. Simply below the surface. That's all that's happening. The progress that you've made is below the surface. Okay, so with that in mind now, as you move someone down the straight line, you ask for the order for the first time, when you engage in your looping, right? So what's the first loop? The first loop is that, okay, I got to knock out more negatives and build up more positives. That's your first loop, right? Then you might step down as for a smaller order, which helps tip the energy in benefits out equation in your favor, right? You ask for the order again. Do they buy maybe? But it's the second pattern where you typically run your action threshold lowering pattern. Where if you build more certainty in the second loop, you now say, let me turn the page now. I say, watch, imagine this is my father. I say exactly what happens. This is my, this is the scale. This is my father. Okay, here is his falcon right over here. Here's his negatives. Here's his positives. You piled on the positives. You piled on the positives. You're eliminating. He's still buying one because he's one or two or three negatives. They're enough to keep the scale from not moving. So when I'm done with my second pattern now, I've just, I built up because I can't listen to this. I can't move his falcon to, you know, here, right? I can't turn it to myself. I can move his falcon. So I want to first, before I try to essentially move this falcon. That's what you do when you lower someone's action threshold, you move the falcon to a more central position. That's what you do. So what happens is I first, before I try to do that, the first thing I'll do though, is I'll be piling on positives, piling on positives, eliminating negatives, eliminating, piling. And then when I'm really got that, so it's maybe only one or two, one negative, two negatives left. Massive positives are built here. And before I ask for the order for the next third time now, I say, Max, let me ask you an honest question. What's the worst that can possibly happen? Let's say I'm wrong. And I, and you know this pattern, and you hijack his strategy of running long, overly negative, overly toxic visuals for the downside. And then you make him step in and your future patient by forcing him using language to run a positive movie on the upside. And what happens as a result of that, you slide this fulcrum back towards the center and boom, the scale plummets down. He goes, fine, I'll do it. That's what is happening when you lower someone's action threshold.
